response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between your WPF project and the .NET 7 runtime on Windows, as WPF is a technology exclusive to the Windows desktop and relies on the older .NET Framework infrastructure. When your .NET 7 application attempts to load the WPF assembly, it is looking for a version that matches the classic .NET Framework 4.0 specifications, but the modern .NET Core or .NET 5+ runtime does not include these legacy dependencies by default in a way that satisfies the specific versioning constraints of the WPF project.

To resolve this without reverting your entire application to .NET Framework, you must ensure that your WPF project is explicitly configured to target .NET Framework 4.8 rather than .NET 7, while keeping your main cross-platform project on .NET 7. This involves creating a separate class library or executable project specifically for the Windows UI that targets the legacy framework, allowing it to load the necessary PresentationFramework assemblies, and then using conditional compilation or reflection to load this specific assembly only when the application is running on Windows.

cousin to this approach is the strategy of utilizing dynamic loading where the main application uses a factory pattern to instantiate the UI layer based on the detected operating system. By isolating the WPF code into a distinct project that targets .NET Framework, you can reference it conditionally in your .NET 7 project using project references that are only active on Windows, or by loading the compiled DLL at runtime via Assembly.LoadFrom if you prefer to keep the reference out of the build process entirely on macOS.

When implementing this separation, you should define a common interface in a shared library that both the WPF implementation and the Cocoa implementation implement, ensuring that your main .NET 7 application interacts only with this abstraction layer. This design pattern allows the Windows build to load the WPF assembly which satisfies the .NET Framework dependency, while the macOS build simply ignores that assembly and loads the native Cocoa code, effectively bypassing the missing file error on non-Windows platforms.

Finally, verify that your build configuration includes the necessary runtime identifiers and that the WPF project is set to produce a DLL or EXE compatible with the .NET Framework 4.8 runtime, ensuring that the file path resolution works correctly when the dynamic loader attempts to find PresentationFramework.
